behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions with a descriptive error message
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is entirely within the parser's own logic — no module handoff is required. The parser takes a string and either returns a schedule object or raises an error. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess. Pure string-to-AST or string-to-struct computation. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. The full failure surface (wrong field range accepted, malformed syntax not caught, error message missing context) is visible at Unit level with parameterized invalid inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Parameterized unit test that feeds known-invalid cron strings (month=13, day=32, hour=25, missing fields, extra fields, non-numeric tokens) into the parser and asserts that each raises the expected error type and that the message contains the offending field/value.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard and edge-case expressions
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the calendar arithmetic inside the calculator — 'last day of month' and 'nearest weekday' are algorithmic edge cases, not module-boundary issues. REAL-INFRA: The calculator operates on datetime objects; no real clock, filesystem, or network is needed — a fixed 'now' value is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock; deterministic inputs produce deterministic outputs. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected because the failure (wrong timestamp) is fully observable by calling the calculator function directly with constructed inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Parameterized unit test providing a fixed reference datetime and a cron expression, then asserting the returned next-N timestamps match hand-computed expected values. Edge cases include last-day-of-month (e.g., '0 0 L * ?'), nearest-weekday (e.g., '0 0 15W * ?'), and year-boundary roll-overs.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies schedules that fire within the configured minimum gap
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The overlap detector's own logic — comparing two lists of fire times against a gap threshold — is the failure boundary. The failure does not require a live handoff between modules; the detector can be called with pre-constructed schedule or fire-time objects. REAL-INFRA: None. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing hidden by mocking here; fire-time lists are passed directly. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered if the detector required live output from the calculator. Behavior text says the detector 'identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap' — the input is two schedule descriptions plus a gap parameter, not a live engine pipeline. Unit is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test constructing pairs of schedule objects (or pre-computed fire-time sequences) and a gap config, then asserting the detector correctly identifies all overlapping windows and reports the conflicting times. Includes cases where gap is exactly met, just inside, and just outside the threshold.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across DST transitions
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the calendar arithmetic when a cron fire time falls in a DST gap or ambiguous fold — this is internal to the calculator's timezone logic. REAL-INFRA: Python's zoneinfo/pytz libraries carry timezone data in-process; no real OS clock, network, or subprocess is required. A fixed UTC 'now' before a known historical DST transition (e.g., America/New_York 2024-03-10) is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: The timezone database must be real (not mocked) to expose the gap/fold arithmetic, but zoneinfo provides that in-process. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered because timezone data lives in the OS tzdata package; however, Python's bundled tzdata or zoneinfo works entirely in-process without OS calls, so System is not required.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test pinning a UTC reference time just before a known spring-forward and a known fall-back transition for a specific IANA timezone. For spring-forward: assert a job scheduled for 2:30 AM either skips to the next valid occurrence or maps to a well-defined resolved time. For fall-back: assert the engine produces exactly one or two results as per its ambiguity policy, and that neither timestamp is outside the valid wall-clock range.

behaviors.description: Engine applies missed-run policy (skip or backfill) when the next run time has already passed
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears at the boundary between the job-config reader (which holds the missed-run policy), the next-run-time calculator (which determines how far in the past the missed fire was), and the engine's dispatch logic (which decides skip vs. backfill). A single-function unit test would either test only one of these components or require so many stubs that the real handoff is invisible. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed; a fake clock with a fixed 'now' and an in-memory job store is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the calculator or the config reader would make the test always pass regardless of whether the engine correctly threads the policy through the pipeline. LLM-DEP: None. MULTI-STEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: Unit was considered but rejected because the behavior explicitly involves the engine reading the policy from job config and then deciding — the failure mode lives at the wiring point, not inside any one function.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test wiring the job-config loader, the next-run-time calculator, and the engine's missed-run policy resolver together with an in-memory job store and a stubbed clock set to a time after the last computed fire time. One job is configured with 'skip' policy and one with 'backfill'. Assert that the skip job produces no catch-up runs and the backfill job enqueues exactly the right number of missed executions.
